common_name: European Chain Fern / Climbing Chain Fern

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in a sheltered, shaded position with consistently moist to wet soil; protect from hard frost in the first winter.

directions: Plant in deep shade in rich, permanently moist to wet soil, such as along a stream bank or at the base of a shaded wall where moisture naturally accumulates. Set the crown at soil level and water thoroughly at planting, then maintain consistent moisture throughout the growing season — this fern will not tolerate drying out. The long arching fronds benefit from shelter from wind, which can damage the tips before they harden. Watch for bulbils forming at the frond tips in late summer; peg them gently to the soil surface and they will root and produce new plantlets, a simple way to propagate this impressive fern.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last hard frost in a sheltered, partially shaded site with humus-rich, reliably moist soil.

directions: Site in partial shade with protection from cold, drying winds, which can damage the emerging fronds that flush in striking shades of copper and burgundy-red in spring. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained but consistently moist soil, incorporating plenty of leaf mold or composted bark at planting to retain moisture and encourage robust growth. The single gem-like bulbil at each frond tip can be pegged to moist compost in late summer to propagate new plants; sever the frond once roots are established. Mulch generously each autumn with leaf mold to protect the crown and feed the soil as it breaks down.

common_name: Little Hard Fern / Alpine Water Fern

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in shade; space closely for quick coverage of difficult ground under trees.

directions: Plant in deep to partial shade in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil, making it an excellent choice for the difficult dry shade under conifers if additional moisture and organic matter are worked in at planting. Set small clump divisions just shallowly enough that the rhizomes sit near the soil surface, spacing them 12 to 18 inches apart; the creeping rhizomes will knit together over time to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat. Keep the soil consistently moist during the first growing season to establish the colony. Avoid alkaline soils and overhead irrigation with hard water, which can cause the fronds to yellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded position; this slow-growing fern benefits from a site with high ambient humidity and minimal disturbance once established.

directions: Plant in deep shade in rich, well-drained but consistently moist, slightly acidic soil, positioning the crown at soil level without burying it — the distinctive rosette form is best appreciated when fully exposed. This is a slow-growing fern requiring patience; avoid disturbing established plants, which resent root disturbance and may take several years to reach full size. Maintain high humidity by mulching with leaf mold around but not over the crown, and site in a naturally sheltered position rather than relying on artificial windbreaks. Protect from frosts below about 23°F (-5°C) with a loose covering of bracken or dry leaves placed over the crown in winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring as new growth begins to emerge; site at the edge of a pond, stream, or boggy area where soil remains consistently wet.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full shade in wet to permanently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil — stream margins, pond edges, and boggy woodland clearings are ideal. Divide large clumps in early spring by splitting the fibrous root mass with a sharp spade; replant divisions promptly and water heavily to prevent them from drying out before new roots establish. The spectacular rusty-brown fertile fronds emerge among the sterile fronds in late spring, providing striking late-season interest before the whole plant turns golden in autumn. Established clumps are very long-lived and develop substantial crowns; avoid moving or splitting unnecessarily once they are settled.

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in late spring once water temperatures have warmed; place in the shallow margins of a pond or stream with 2 to 6 inches of water over the crown.

directions: Plant tubers or divided clumps into heavy loam or aquatic planting compost in a basket or directly into the muddy margins of a pond or water feature, with 2 to 6 inches of water covering the crown. Site in full sun for the best flowering — the large white three-petaled blooms appear in summer and are most prolific in warm, bright conditions. Divide congested clumps every two to three years in spring to maintain vigorous growth and encourage generous flowering. In zones below 8, lift and overwinter tubers in damp sand in a frost-free location, replanting the following spring once water temperatures exceed 65°F.
